result:
:::writing
There’s a particular kind of quiet that only shows up just before something changes. It’s not silence, exactly. It hums. Like the moment before rain when the air feels heavier, as if the sky is thinking. People often miss it because they’re busy looking for noise, for obvious signs, for something that announces itself with certainty.

But change is rarely polite like that.

It slips in through small cracks. A conversation that lingers longer than expected. A new idea that refuses to leave. A sudden discomfort with something that used to feel fine. None of these seem important on their own, and yet they rearrange things slowly, like furniture being moved in the dark.

I think we underestimate how much of life is shaped in these in-between spaces. Not the big decisions we announce, but the quiet shifts we barely notice. The subtle courage it takes to reconsider, to outgrow, to admit that something no longer fits.

There’s a strange beauty in that uncertainty. It means something is still alive, still in motion. Certainty, for all its comfort, can be a kind of stillness. And stillness, left too long, turns into stagnation.

So maybe the goal isn’t to chase clarity all the time. Maybe it’s to get better at recognizing that quiet hum, and not rushing to silence it. To sit with it, even when it feels inconvenient.

Because more often than not, that quiet is the beginning of something trying to become.
:::

content.text:
:::writing
There’s a particular kind of quiet that only shows up just before something changes. It’s not silence, exactly. It hums. Like the moment before rain when the air feels heavier, as if the sky is thinking. People often miss it because they’re busy looking for noise, for obvious signs, for something that announces itself with certainty.

But change is rarely polite like that.

It slips in through small cracks. A conversation that lingers longer than expected. A new idea that refuses to leave. A sudden discomfort with something that used to feel fine. None of these seem important on their own, and yet they rearrange things slowly, like furniture being moved in the dark.

I think we underestimate how much of life is shaped in these in-between spaces. Not the big decisions we announce, but the quiet shifts we barely notice. The subtle courage it takes to reconsider, to outgrow, to admit that something no longer fits.

There’s a strange beauty in that uncertainty. It means something is still alive, still in motion. Certainty, for all its comfort, can be a kind of stillness. And stillness, left too long, turns into stagnation.

So maybe the goal isn’t to chase clarity all the time. Maybe it’s to get better at recognizing that quiet hum, and not rushing to silence it. To sit with it, even when it feels inconvenient.

Because more often than not, that quiet is the beginning of something trying to become.
:::
